Scenario_Master.Description: A short day VFR local flight with one or more passengers. Emphasis on pilot qualifications, passenger briefings, documents, W&B, weather, airspace, and ADM.
A planned VFR cross-country requiring route selection, weather evaluation, fuel planning, performance, airspace, navigation, and diversion decisions.
A VFR flight between islands or over water with limited alternates. Emphasis on overwater planning, weather, survival equipment, fuel reserves, alternates, and risk management.
A flight where weather, NOTAMs, or destination conditions deteriorate. Emphasis on weather products, personal minimums, diversion, fuel decision points, and external pressure.
A night VFR return flight with passenger-currency, lighting, visual illusions, electrical, weather, and emergency considerations.

Evaluation_Segment_Master.Description: Pilot certificates, medical privileges, currency, privileges/limitations, passenger carrying, endorsements.
Required documents, inspections, maintenance records, ADs, inoperative equipment, return-to-service.
Weather sources, forecasts, AIRMETs/SIGMETs, cloud/visibility requirements, go/no-go decisions.
Route, altitude, checkpoints, nav log, NOTAMs, alternates, fuel stops, preflight planning.
Loading, CG, passenger/cargo decisions, aircraft limitations, W&B computations.
Takeoff/landing distance, density altitude, climb, runway surface/slope, POH data use.
Airspace, ATC services, radio procedures, lost communications, ADS-B/transponder.
Pilotage, dead reckoning, VOR/GPS, position awareness, lost procedures.
PAVE/5P/IMSAFE, external pressure, passenger management, personal minimums, PIC authority.
Electrical, fuel, engine, flight controls, avionics, abnormal system indications.
Engine failure, fire, emergency approach/landing, emergency equipment, distress communications.
When and how to divert, alternate selection, fuel and weather decision points, runway suitability.
Night currency, lighting, illusions, night weather, night emergencies, night airport operations.
Fatigue, alcohol, hypoxia, spatial disorientation, stress, visual scanning, aeromedical factors.
Taxi, runway incursions, signs/markings, wake turbulence, ramp safety, collision avoidance.

Event_Master.Description: The primary GPS or tablet navigation source fails enroute.
The alternator or low-voltage light indicates an electrical charging problem.
A passenger becomes airsick, anxious, or otherwise uncomfortable.
Weather deteriorates below forecast or below personal minimums.
A NOTAM or ATC update indicates the planned runway or airport is unavailable.
Actual fuel burn, headwind, delay, or diversion creates a fuel planning concern.
A radio or communication problem occurs near controlled airspace.
An equipment discrepancy is discovered before departure.
Temperature, elevation, or loading significantly affects performance.
Passengers apply pressure to depart, continue, or complete the flight.
The pilot encounters night illusions, dark terrain, or reduced visual references.
The engine begins running rough or abnormal indications are observed.

Question_Bank.Question: What certificates and documents must you have on your person before acting as PIC for this scenario? (Dinner Flight With Friends)
Question_Bank.Correct_Answer: The applicant should identify pilot certificate, government photo ID, and current medical qualification or BasicMed, plus any required endorsements or logbook evidence if applicable.
Question_Bank.Common_Errors: Rote answer without applying the scenario; failing to state a clear PIC decision; missing legal vs safe distinction.

Question_Bank.Question: Are you current to carry passengers in this scenario, and how would you prove it? (Dinner Flight With Friends)
Question_Bank.Correct_Answer: The applicant must show compliance with recent flight experience: three takeoffs and landings in the same category/class/type if required within the preceding 90 days; at night, full-stop landings during the period from one hour after sunset to one hour before sunrise. You would document this in your logbook.,
Question_Bank.Common_Errors: Rote answer without applying the scenario; failing to state a clear PIC decision; missing legal vs safe distinction.

Question_Bank.Question: What private pilot privileges and limitations apply if your friend offers to pay for the flight? (Dinner Flight With Friends)
Question_Bank.Correct_Answer: A private pilot may not act as PIC for compensation or hire and may only share operating expenses pro rata with passengers for fuel, oil, airport expenses, or rental fees when the flight has a common purpose.
Question_Bank.Common_Errors: Rote answer without applying the scenario; failing to state a clear PIC decision; missing legal vs safe distinction.

Question_Bank.Question: Does your pilot certificate expire? What about your medical privileges? (Dinner Flight With Friends)
Question_Bank.Correct_Answer: A private pilot certificate generally does not expire, but medical privileges expire based on certificate class and age, unless operating under BasicMed within its limitations.

Question_Bank.Question: What is the risk of inadvertent IMC for a VFR pilot? (Hood Work and Unusual Attitude)

Question_Bank.Question: How do you recover from a nose-high unusual attitude? (Hood Work and Unusual Attitude)

Question_Bank.Question: How do you recover from a nose-low unusual attitude? (Hood Work and Unusual Attitude)

Question_Bank.Question: What should you tell ATC if you inadvertently enter IMC? (Hood Work and Unusual Attitude)
